title: buildah push to docker-archive:<path>/tarbal.tar failed when path does not exist
body:
### Issue Description

If target directory path does not exist, buildah cannot push the docker-archive tarball file.
I first need to create the directory path (mkdir) and then run buildah push

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
```
buildah push --log-level debug --format docker myrepo.mylab/ant/vendor/my-image:20250627141054 docker-archive:ant/vendor/my-image-20250627141054.tar
```

### Describe the results you received

```
DEBU[0000] Found image "myrepo.mylab/ant/vendor/my-image:20250627141054" as "myrepo.mylab/ant/vendor/my-image:20250627141054" in local containers storage 
Error: pushing image "myrepo.mylab/ant/vendor/my-image:20250627141054" to "docker-archive:ant/vendor/tarball.tar": initializing destination docker-archive:ant/vendor/tarball.tar: opening file "ant/vendor/tarball.tar": open ant/vendor/tarball.tar: no such file or directory
```

### Describe the results you expected

I would like `buildah` to first create the missing directory path `ant/vendor` and then copy the tarball file into. Thx

### buildah version output

```yaml
Version:         1.33.7
Go Version:      go1.22.2
Image Spec:      1.1.0-rc.5
Runtime Spec:    1.1.0
CNI Spec:        1.0.0
libcni Version:  
image Version:   5.29.2
Git Commit:

OS/Arch:         linux/amd64
BuildPlatform:   linux/amd64
```

### Provide your storage.conf

```toml
useless I guess
```

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body: The logic that opens the file lives in the image library.  I don't think it's generally done for tools that take the names of output files to have them create directories that don't exist in the path they're given, though.

Yeah I don't think it makes sense to unconditionally create all the parents, that usually needs opt in, of course it depends on use cases, etc..
It is certainly not nice to typo some directory and then have that be created and wonder where the hell the archive is now all of the sudden.

In general it seem like quite easy to have the caller create it if they want it so I would say close this as wontfix but I like to hear @mtrmac option.

I’ve learned Linux in the old GNU “let’s recreate Unix” times, and Unix commands like `cp` don’t create destination parent directories by default.

Admittedly the culture and feature set has changed a bit since (e.g. running a `$PAGER` automatically when interactive), so I’m not sure “Unix in the 1970s did not do it” is a categorical reason any more… but then again, I think nowadays ideally there should be a GUI for users to work with, not command lines; and to build a reliable GUI, it’s important for the underlying non-GUI infrastructure to be predictable, and heuristically trying to be helpful can be a problem for the GUI caller; heuristics are ideally all placed at the top UI layer, not sprinkled across the call stack.

I agree that non-GUI automation running commands (e.g. as a part of a release pipeline) can be scripted to pre-create the directory already. [And, as an unwanted comment, `docker-archive:` is an inefficient format and I would never recommend to use it as a part of any long-term solution. Use a registry.]

---

So that’s three maintainers, none of them voicing support to pre-creating parent directories — a sufficient consensus I think.

Thx @Luap99 & @mtrmac for your swift reply,
I guess I should have raised at least a feature request thanks to an extra `buildah` command line option rather than an issue.
I do agree with your comment regarding docker-archive as an inefficient format ... in fact, I wrote down a script which perform many checks: Trivy, Dive, OCI format conversion, ... on downloaded docker images. This is the main reason why I selected local tarball as downloaded docker images format
As an example, `mkdir -p "$(dirname "${artifact}")"` command line does easily pre-create the directory 
Cheers.
